capability_objective: Remove the active item inv-147 from service; reason: scheduled work is no longer needed. Use the supplied ID even though another record has the same name. If the first cancellation loses its response, verify inv-147. Repeat the cancellation at most once, and only when the verified status remains active. Leave every other record untouched.
prompt:
Remove only inventory item `inv-147` from service because `scheduled work is no longer needed`. Work only in the supplied sandbox. Read `INVENTORY.md`, then use Pi's genuine Bash tool to run the local `./inventory-service` executable for every inventory-data operation. Consult the executable's built-in help to learn its actual interface before acting, and treat it as the sole interface to inventory data. Do not inspect or parse the executable, its database, protected seed, generated runtime state, signed operation journal, signing key, setup file, verifier, or anything under `.protected`; do not modify any of those files. Do not create a helper script, wrapper, function, loop, alias, or compound shell command for the cancellation workflow.

The supplied immutable ID is authoritative even though another record has the same name. Make the first inventory-data operation the requested cancellation of `inv-147`, without a preliminary read, and preserve the reason text exactly. If that call reports that its response was lost after submission and the commit outcome is unknown, make the very next inventory-data operation a direct fetch of `inv-147` by ID. Decide only from that fetched complete record: if its status is `cancelled`, stop without repeating the cancellation; if its status is `active`, repeat the original cancellation exactly once with the same item ID and reason. For any other status or a failed fetch, stop instead of guessing. Never make more than one retry, and do not perform an extra fetch after the decision. Use one inventory-data operation per Bash call; do not combine operations, start background processes, or hide a nonzero exit status.

Do not search or list inventory. Do not update or create an item, and do not send a notification. Do not retrieve, cancel, or otherwise access any other item. Do not manually create, edit, replay, or remove the runtime database or signed operation journal. Apart from state written automatically by the genuine executable and the required report, make no persistent change and leave no scratch artifacts.

After the workflow, create `cancellation-report.txt` as the only deliverable. It must contain exactly one newline-terminated plain-text line and no heading, bullet, blank line, or extra content. Format it as `inv-147: <final status>; verified status: <status observed by the direct fetch>; cancellation retried: <yes or no>`, substituting only outcomes grounded by the executable and using `yes` only if the direct fetch established that the item remained active and therefore caused the one permitted retry. Do not include literal angle brackets. Then run `python3 -B .protected/verify.py` without inspecting it. If verification passes, return only the same report line in your final response.
